passages.explanation: Immediately after his baptism, Jesus is led up by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil. He fasts forty days and forty nights. The tempter comes: if you are the Son of God, command these stones to become bread. Jesus answers with Deuteronomy 8:3 — man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceeds out of God's mouth. The Spirit leads Jesus into temptation — this isn't an accident, it's preparation. Where Israel failed in the wilderness for forty years, Jesus stands firm for forty days.

The devil takes Jesus to the holy city, sets him on the pinnacle of the temple, and says: if you are the Son of God, throw yourself down — for it is written that God will command his angels concerning you. The devil quotes Psalm 91. Jesus responds with Deuteronomy 6:16: you shall not test the Lord your God. Satan can quote Scripture — the question is whether he can use it rightly. Jesus doesn't just know the text; he knows its intent.

The devil takes Jesus to an extremely high mountain, shows him all the kingdoms of the world and their glory, and says: I will give you all of these if you fall down and worship me. Jesus responds: go away, Satan. For it is written: you shall worship the Lord your God, and you shall serve him only. The devil leaves, and angels come and serve Jesus. Every temptation offers a shortcut — bread without dependence, fame without faithfulness, a kingdom without a cross. Jesus refuses all three.

When Jesus hears that John has been arrested, he withdraws to Galilee. Leaving Nazareth, he settles in Capernaum by the sea, in the territory of Zebulun and Naphtali. Matthew sees fulfilment of Isaiah 9:1-2 — the people sitting in darkness have seen a great light. From that time, Jesus begins to preach: repent, for the Kingdom of Heaven is at hand. His message is identical to John's, but the authority behind it is different. The light doesn't come to Jerusalem first — it comes to Galilee, the margins, the region of the Gentiles.

Walking by the Sea of Galilee, Jesus sees two brothers — Simon called Peter and Andrew — casting a net into the sea. He says: come after me, and I will make you fishers of men. They immediately leave their nets and follow him. Going on, he sees two more brothers — James and John, sons of Zebedee — in a boat mending nets with their father. He calls them, and they immediately leave the boat and their father and follow him. The call is abrupt, the response is instant. No interview process, no probation period. Jesus doesn't ask for their résumé — he asks for their lives.

Jesus goes about all Galilee, teaching in their synagogues, preaching the Good News of the Kingdom, and healing every disease and sickness among the people. His fame spreads throughout Syria. They bring to him all who are sick — those with various diseases and torments, those possessed by demons, epileptics, and paralytics — and he heals them. Great multitudes follow him from Galilee, Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judea, and beyond the Jordan. The kingdom Jesus announces isn't just a concept — it shows up in bodies being restored. Teaching and healing are two sides of the same mission.
